title: Mesothelioma
oneLiner: Aggressive asbestos-related pleural/peritoneal malignancy; presents with breathlessness and chest pain; median survival <12 months — early symptom control is key.

sections.Killers First — What Will Kill Them In T:
- **Massive pleural effusion with mediastinal shift:** Sats <90% on 15 L O2, tracheal deviation away from side of effusion, hypotension — needs immediate therapeutic thoracentesis (1–1.5 L max) or chest drain.
- **Superior vena cava obstruction (SVCO) from mediastinal involvement:** Face/neck swelling, dilated chest veins, stridor — needs urgent CT chest + oncology review; consider dexamethasone 16 mg IV bolus (after CT confirmed) [check local policy].
- **Pulmonary embolism (PE) – coagulopathy or thrombosis:** Sudden pleuritic chest pain + haemoptysis + hypoxia out of proportion to effusion — check Wells score, d-dimer if low probability, otherwise CTPA.
sections.Presentation:
- **Cardinal symptoms:**  
  - Progressive dyspnoea (most common)  
  - Unilateral dull chest pain (often non-pleuritic, continuous)  
  - Cough, weight loss, fatigue  
  - Night sweats, finger clubbing (late)  
- **Key exam findings:**  
  - Stony dull percussion + reduced breath sounds + decreased chest expansion (pleural effusion)  
  - Cachexia, clubbing, rarely Horner’s syndrome (apical involvement)  
  - Abdominal mass / ascites if peritoneal disease
sections.Differential Diagnosis:
- **Most likely → less likely:**  
  - **Benign asbestos pleural disease:** pleural plaques, no mass, no weight loss — CT shows calcified plaques, no nodular thickening.  
  - **Lung cancer (primary or metastatic):** smoking history, central vs peripheral lesion, sputum cytology, bronchoscopy.  
  - **Pleural infection (empyema):** fever, purulent effusion, CRP >100, neutrophils on fluid.  
  - **Other metastatic pleural disease (breast, ovary, lymphoma):** known primary, bilateral effusion, cytology shows other cell type.  
- **Splitter:**  
  - **Contrast-enhanced CT chest:** If pleural thickening is nodular, >1 cm circumferential, and involves mediastinal pleura → highly suggestive of mesothelioma. Benign disease shows smooth, calcified plaques without soft tissue mass.
sections.Investigations:
- **Bedside:**  
  - ECG (check for PE, arrhythmia, SVCO)  
  - Pulse oximetry, respiratory rate, NEWS2  
  - Chest ultrasound if available – loculated vs free-flowing effusion  
- **Bloods:**  
  - FBC, U&E, LFT, bone profile (hypercalcaemia rare), CRP, LDH  
  - Clotting screen (before pleural procedure)  
  - Tumour markers: Mesothelin (not diagnostic but supports), but not routine  
  - d-dimer if PE suspected  
- **Imaging:**  
  - **First-line:** CT chest with contrast (whole thorax + upper abdomen) – assess pleural thickening, effusion, mediastinal nodes, metastases  
  - **Second-line:** PET-CT for staging (extent of disease, surgical candidacy)  
  - MRI for diaphragm/chest wall invasion (if surgery considered)  
- **Special tests:**  
  - **Pleural aspiration:** send for cytology, protein, LDH, pH, microbiological culture. Ideally at least 50 mL fresh (no formalin).  
  - **Closed pleural biopsy (Abrams needle)** – low yield.  
  - **Thoracoscopy (VATS) with biopsy** – gold standard for adequate tissue for histology + immunohistochemistry (calretinin, WT-1, cytokeratin 5/6).  
- **Diagnostic criteria:** Histological + immunohistochemical confirmation. No blood test diagnostic.  
- **Never-miss test:** **Thoracoscopic pleural biopsy** – if only fluid cytology sent, false negative rate >50%. Inadequate tissue means delay in diagnosis and missed treatment window.
sections.Management:
- **Immediate (first hour / ED):**  
  - Oxygen to target sats 94-98%  
  - If respiratory distress from large effusion → therapeutic thoracentesis (max 1.5 L) or chest drain (under ultrasound guidance if possible)  
  - Analgesia: paracetamol + codeine or morphine for breakthrough  
  - SVCO: dexamethasone 16 mg IV (after CT) + oncology referral  
- **Ward (first 24-72h):**  
  - Complete diagnostic workup: CT + biopsy (VATS if fit)  
  - Pleurodesis if recurrent effusion and good lung expansion (talc slurry via drain or poudrage at thoracoscopy)  
  - Insert indwelling pleural catheter (IPC) if trapped lung or poor performance status  
- **Long-term / outpatient:**  
  - MDT discussion: oncology, thoracic surgery, palliative care  
  - Chemotherapy: first-line pemetrexed + cisplatin (doses per local oncology protocol; typically pemetrexed 500 mg/m² + cisplatin 75 mg/m² IV q21d)  
  - Radiotherapy for pain control or prophylaxis (biopsy tract irradiation)  
  - Palliative support: pain team, physiotherapy, nutritional support  
  - Consider immunotherapy or clinical trial if fit  
- **Default Orders (if admitted):**  
  - Fluids: 0.9% sodium chloride 125 mL/hr unless NBM (then adjust for maintenance)  
  - VTE prophylaxis: enoxaparin 40 mg SC daily (if no bleeding, normal renal function) – check full risk assessment  
  - Diet: normal oral intake unless NBM for biopsy  
  - Observations: 4-hourly NEWS – increase to hourly if unstable
sections.Drugs & Doses:
- **First-line (chemotherapy):**  
  - Pemetrexed 500 mg/m² IV + cisplatin 75 mg/m² IV day 1, every 21 days for 4-6 cycles – [**check local oncology protocol – pre-medication with folic acid + B12 required**]  
- **Common alternatives:**  
  - Carboplatin AUC 5 IV (if cisplatin contraindicated – renal impairment, neuropathy)  
  - Maintenance pemetrexed alone after initial cycles (in some centres)  
- **Supportive:**  
  - Dexamethasone: 16 mg IV bolus for SVCO then 4 mg PO QDS tapering  
  - Morphine: 2.5–5 mg SC PRN for severe pain (max 2-hourly)  
- **Key contraindications / interactions:**  
  - Cisplatin: nephrotoxic – ensure adequate hydration, monitor creatinine; avoid other nephrotoxics (NSAIDs, gentamicin)  
  - Pemetrexed: avoid NSAIDs (reduce renal excretion) – use paracetamol or weak opioids  
- **Renal/hepatic adjustment:**  
  - Cisplatin: eGFR <60 → reduce dose or use carboplatin; creatinine >1.5 mg/dL → delay  
  - Pemetrexed: no dose reduction if CrCl >45 mL/min; contraindicated if CrCl <45 mL/min  
- **Most dangerous dose error:**  
  - **Pemetrexed without B12 and folic acid supplementation** – leads to severe myelosuppression and diarrhoea – always ensure patient has been on folic acid 400 mcg PO daily and B12 1000 mcg IM q9 weeks for at least 1 week before first dose.
sections.Complications:
- **Acute:**  
  - Respiratory failure from large effusion or SVCO  
  - Empyema after drainage – fever, purulent fluid, rising CRP – start IV antibiotics (e.g. co-amoxiclav + metronidazole) and drain completely  
  - Chemotherapy side effects: febrile neutropenia, nephrotoxicity, nausea/vomiting, mucositis  
  - Pneumothorax after biopsy/drain insertion  
- **Chronic:**  
  - Recurrent pleural effusion (trapped lung) – IPC placement  
  - Cachexia, chronic chest wall pain – need early palliative care  
  - Peritoneal involvement (if primary peritoneal disease) – ascites, bowel obstruction  
  - SVCO recurrence – treat with radiotherapy/stent
sections.Prognosis:
- Median survival from diagnosis: 8–14 months (epithelioid subtype best, sarcomatoid worst).  
- >80% die within 2 years. Surgery (pleurectomy/decortication) offers modest survival benefit in highly selected patients.  
- 30-day mortality from diagnosis is low unless SVCO/empyema. Most die from respiratory failure or progressive cachexia.
sections.Pearls (positive actions):
- **Always ask about asbestos exposure** – mandatory in history; document occupational history (building, shipyard, manufacturing).  
- **Send pleural fluid in a fresh sterile pot (NOT formalin)** for cytology – add heparin to prevent clotting.  
- **Refer early to palliative care** – symptom control (pain, breathlessness, cough) improves quality of life.  
- **Consider indwelling pleural catheter** for recurrent effusion – avoids repeated aspiration and reduces hospital visits.
sections.Pitfalls (Errors To Avoid):
- **Do not accept a negative pleural fluid cytology as proof of benign disease** – sensitivity <30%; always push for tissue biopsy (VATS).  
- **Do not use NSAIDs for pain in patients on pemetrexed** – increases toxicity; use paracetamol + opioids.  
- **Do not drain >1.5 L of pleural fluid in one sitting** – risk of re-expansion pulmonary oedema. If large volume, drain slowly or use a controlled system.  
- **Do not delay MDT discussion** – even with symptoms, deciding on treatment approach (pleurodesis vs IPC, chemotherapy vs palliation) is complex.
sections.Safety Netting (If Patient Goes Home):
- **Return if:**  
  - Sudden worsening breathlessness, chest pain, or fever (pleural infection)  
  - Blood in sputum or coughing up fluid  
  - Signs of SVCO: facial swelling, difficulty swallowing, stridor  
  - Chemotherapy side effects: fever >38°C (neutropenia), persistent vomiting, diarrhoea, mouth ulcers  
- **Follow-up:**  
  - Respiratory/oncology clinic within 2 weeks of diagnosis  
  - If IPC inserted – district nurse for drain care, review weekly  
  - If on chemotherapy – bloods (FBC, U&E, LFT) before each cycle; call if neutrophils <1.0 or platelets <100
sections.Escalation Criteria:
- **Call senior / oncology registrar if:**  
  - NEWS2 ≥7 or sustained ≥5  
  - Oxygen requirement >4 L/min to maintain sats >90%  
  - Septic criteria (SIRS + suspected infection)  
  - Acute SVCO with stridor or airway compromise  
  - Falling consciousness / GCS <15  
  - Metabolic acidosis (lactate >2 mmol/L)  
- **Call crash team if:**  
  - GCS <8 or airway compromise  
  - Cardiac/respiratory arrest  
  - Systolic BP <90 despite fluids  
- **Urgent oncology advice if:**  
  - First cycle of pemetrexed not preceded by B12/folic acid (risk of severe toxicity)  
  - Febrile neutropenia (temp >38°C, neutrophils <0.5) – start IV piperacillin-tazobactam per local policy

rawMarkdown:
## Mesothelioma
**One-liner:** Aggressive asbestos-related pleural/peritoneal malignancy; presents with breathlessness and chest pain; median survival <12 months — early symptom control is key.

### Killers First — What Will Kill Them In The First Hour?
- **Massive pleural effusion with mediastinal shift:** Sats <90% on 15 L O2, tracheal deviation away from side of effusion, hypotension — needs immediate therapeutic thoracentesis (1–1.5 L max) or chest drain.
- **Superior vena cava obstruction (SVCO) from mediastinal involvement:** Face/neck swelling, dilated chest veins, stridor — needs urgent CT chest + oncology review; consider dexamethasone 16 mg IV bolus (after CT confirmed) [check local policy].
- **Pulmonary embolism (PE) – coagulopathy or thrombosis:** Sudden pleuritic chest pain + haemoptysis + hypoxia out of proportion to effusion — check Wells score, d-dimer if low probability, otherwise CTPA.

### Presentation
- **Cardinal symptoms:**  
  - Progressive dyspnoea (most common)  
  - Unilateral dull chest pain (often non-pleuritic, continuous)  
  - Cough, weight loss, fatigue  
  - Night sweats, finger clubbing (late)  
- **Key exam findings:**  
  - Stony dull percussion + reduced breath sounds + decreased chest expansion (pleural effusion)  
  - Cachexia, clubbing, rarely Horner’s syndrome (apical involvement)  
  - Abdominal mass / ascites if peritoneal disease

### Differential Diagnosis
- **Most likely → less likely:**  
  - **Benign asbestos pleural disease:** pleural plaques, no mass, no weight loss — CT shows calcified plaques, no nodular thickening.  
  - **Lung cancer (primary or metastatic):** smoking history, central vs peripheral lesion, sputum cytology, bronchoscopy.  
  - **Pleural infection (empyema):** fever, purulent effusion, CRP >100, neutrophils on fluid.  
  - **Other metastatic pleural disease (breast, ovary, lymphoma):** known primary, bilateral effusion, cytology shows other cell type.  
- **Splitter:**  
  - **Contrast-enhanced CT chest:** If pleural thickening is nodular, >1 cm circumferential, and involves mediastinal pleura → highly suggestive of mesothelioma. Benign disease shows smooth, calcified plaques without soft tissue mass.

### Investigations
- **Bedside:**  
  - ECG (check for PE, arrhythmia, SVCO)  
  - Pulse oximetry, respiratory rate, NEWS2  
  - Chest ultrasound if available – loculated vs free-flowing effusion  
- **Bloods:**  
  - FBC, U&E, LFT, bone profile (hypercalcaemia rare), CRP, LDH  
  - Clotting screen (before pleural procedure)  
  - Tumour markers: Mesothelin (not diagnostic but supports), but not routine  
  - d-dimer if PE suspected  
- **Imaging:**  
  - **First-line:** CT chest with contrast (whole thorax + upper abdomen) – assess pleural thickening, effusion, mediastinal nodes, metastases  
  - **Second-line:** PET-CT for staging (extent of disease, surgical candidacy)  
  - MRI for diaphragm/chest wall invasion (if surgery considered)  
- **Special tests:**  
  - **Pleural aspiration:** send for cytology, protein, LDH, pH, microbiological culture. Ideally at least 50 mL fresh (no formalin).  
  - **Closed pleural biopsy (Abrams needle)** – low yield.  
  - **Thoracoscopy (VATS) with biopsy** – gold standard for adequate tissue for histology + immunohistochemistry (calretinin, WT-1, cytokeratin 5/6).  
- **Diagnostic criteria:** Histological + immunohistochemical confirmation. No blood test diagnostic.  
- **Never-miss test:** **Thoracoscopic pleural biopsy** – if only fluid cytology sent, false negative rate >50%. Inadequate tissue means delay in diagnosis and missed treatment window.

### Management
- **Immediate (first hour / ED):**  
  - Oxygen to target sats 94-98%  
  - If respiratory distress from large effusion → therapeutic thoracentesis (max 1.5 L) or chest drain (under ultrasound guidance if possible)  
  - Analgesia: paracetamol + codeine or morphine for breakthrough  
  - SVCO: dexamethasone 16 mg IV (after CT) + oncology referral  
- **Ward (first 24-72h):**  
  - Complete diagnostic workup: CT + biopsy (VATS if fit)  
  - Pleurodesis if recurrent effusion and good lung expansion (talc slurry via drain or poudrage at thoracoscopy)  
  - Insert indwelling pleural catheter (IPC) if trapped lung or poor performance status  
- **Long-term / outpatient:**  
  - MDT discussion: oncology, thoracic surgery, palliative care  
  - Chemotherapy: first-line pemetrexed + cisplatin (doses per local oncology protocol; typically pemetrexed 500 mg/m² + cisplatin 75 mg/m² IV q21d)  
  - Radiotherapy for pain control or prophylaxis (biopsy tract irradiation)  
  - Palliative support: pain team, physiotherapy, nutritional support  
  - Consider immunotherapy or clinical trial if fit  
- **Default Orders (if admitted):**  
  - Fluids: 0.9% sodium chloride 125 mL/hr unless NBM (then adjust for maintenance)  
  - VTE prophylaxis: enoxaparin 40 mg SC daily (if no bleeding, normal renal function) – check full risk assessment  
  - Diet: normal oral intake unless NBM for biopsy  
  - Observations: 4-hourly NEWS – increase to hourly if unstable

### Drugs & Doses
- **First-line (chemotherapy):**  
  - Pemetrexed 500 mg/m² IV + cisplatin 75 mg/m² IV day 1, every 21 days for 4-6 cycles – [**check local oncology protocol – pre-medication with folic acid + B12 required**]  
- **Common alternatives:**  
  - Carboplatin AUC 5 IV (if cisplatin contraindicated – renal impairment, neuropathy)  
  - Maintenance pemetrexed alone after initial cycles (in some centres)  
- **Supportive:**  
  - Dexamethasone: 16 mg IV bolus for SVCO then 4 mg PO QDS tapering  
  - Morphine: 2.5–5 mg SC PRN for severe pain (max 2-hourly)  
- **Key contraindications / interactions:**  
  - Cisplatin: nephrotoxic – ensure adequate hydration, monitor creatinine; avoid other nephrotoxics (NSAIDs, gentamicin)  
  - Pemetrexed: avoid NSAIDs (reduce renal excretion) – use paracetamol or weak opioids  
- **Renal/hepatic adjustment:**  
  - Cisplatin: eGFR <60 → reduce dose or use carboplatin; creatinine >1.5 mg/dL → delay  
  - Pemetrexed: no dose reduction if CrCl >45 mL/min; contraindicated if CrCl <45 mL/min  
- **Most dangerous dose error:**  
  - **Pemetrexed without B12 and folic acid supplementation** – leads to severe myelosuppression and diarrhoea – always ensure patient has been on folic acid 400 mcg PO daily and B12 1000 mcg IM q9 weeks for at least 1 week before first dose.

### Complications
- **Acute:**  
  - Respiratory failure from large effusion or SVCO  
  - Empyema after drainage – fever, purulent fluid, rising CRP – start IV antibiotics (e.g. co-amoxiclav + metronidazole) and drain completely  
  - Chemotherapy side effects: febrile neutropenia, nephrotoxicity, nausea/vomiting, mucositis  
  - Pneumothorax after biopsy/drain insertion  
- **Chronic:**  
  - Recurrent pleural effusion (trapped lung) – IPC placement  
  - Cachexia, chronic chest wall pain – need early palliative care  
  - Peritoneal involvement (if primary peritoneal disease) – ascites, bowel obstruction  
  - SVCO recurrence – treat with radiotherapy/stent

### Prognosis
- Median survival from diagnosis: 8–14 months (epithelioid subtype best, sarcomatoid worst).  
- >80% die within 2 years. Surgery (pleurectomy/decortication) offers modest survival benefit in highly selected patients.  
- 30-day mortality from diagnosis is low unless SVCO/empyema. Most die from respiratory failure or progressive cachexia.

### Pearls (positive actions)
- **Always ask about asbestos exposure** – mandatory in history; document occupational history (building, shipyard, manufacturing).  
- **Send pleural fluid in a fresh sterile pot (NOT formalin)** for cytology – add heparin to prevent clotting.  
- **Refer early to palliative care** – symptom control (pain, breathlessness, cough) improves quality of life.  
- **Consider indwelling pleural catheter** for recurrent effusion – avoids repeated aspiration and reduces hospital visits.

### Pitfalls (Errors To Avoid)
- **Do not accept a negative pleural fluid cytology as proof of benign disease** – sensitivity <30%; always push for tissue biopsy (VATS).  
- **Do not use NSAIDs for pain in patients on pemetrexed** – increases toxicity; use paracetamol + opioids.  
- **Do not drain >1.5 L of pleural fluid in one sitting** – risk of re-expansion pulmonary oedema. If large volume, drain slowly or use a controlled system.  
- **Do not delay MDT discussion** – even with symptoms, deciding on treatment approach (pleurodesis vs IPC, chemotherapy vs palliation) is complex.

### Safety Netting (If Patient Goes Home)
- **Return if:**  
  - Sudden worsening breathlessness, chest pain, or fever (pleural infection)  
  - Blood in sputum or coughing up fluid  
  - Signs of SVCO: facial swelling, difficulty swallowing, stridor  
  - Chemotherapy side effects: fever >38°C (neutropenia), persistent vomiting, diarrhoea, mouth ulcers  
- **Follow-up:**  
  - Respiratory/oncology clinic within 2 weeks of diagnosis  
  - If IPC inserted – district nurse for drain care, review weekly  
  - If on chemotherapy – bloods (FBC, U&E, LFT) before each cycle; call if neutrophils <1.0 or platelets <100

### Escalation Criteria
- **Call senior / oncology registrar if:**  
  - NEWS2 ≥7 or sustained ≥5  
  - Oxygen requirement >4 L/min to maintain sats >90%  
  - Septic criteria (SIRS + suspected infection)  
  - Acute SVCO with stridor or airway compromise  
  - Falling consciousness / GCS <15  
  - Metabolic acidosis (lactate >2 mmol/L)  
- **Call crash team if:**  
  - GCS <8 or airway compromise  
  - Cardiac/respiratory arrest  
  - Systolic BP <90 despite fluids  
- **Urgent oncology advice if:**  
  - First cycle of pemetrexed not preceded by B12/folic acid (risk of severe toxicity)  
  - Febrile neutropenia (temp >38°C, neutrophils <0.5) – start IV piperacillin-tazobactam per local policy
